real_meaning: ～ながら expresses that two actions happen simultaneously, with the nuance that the ～ながら action is the secondary or background activity while the main verb is the primary focus. The speaker is highlighting that something else is going on 'at the same time' — it's not just listing two actions, it's framing one as the backdrop for the other. There's also a completely separate usage meaning 'even though / despite,' which carries a sense of contradiction or irony.

formation.rule: Attach ながら to the verb STEM (ます-stem) of the background action. The main action comes at the end of the sentence. Example: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べる → <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べ + ながら, <ruby>歩<rt>ある</rt></ruby>く → <ruby>歩<rt>ある</rt></ruby>き + ながら, する → し + ながら. For the 'despite' meaning, it can attach to い-adjective stems, な-adjective stems, or nouns: <ruby>知<rt>し</rt></ruby>りながら ('while knowing/even though knowing').
formation.common_mistake: Learners often mistakenly attach ながら to the dictionary form or て-form instead of the verb stem. Wrong: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べてながら / <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるながら. Correct: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べながら.

sentences.jp: <ruby>音楽<rt>おんがく</rt></ruby><ruby>聴<rt>き</rt></ruby>きながら<ruby>宿題<rt>しゅくだい</rt></ruby>やってるんだけど、<ruby>全然<rt>ぜんぜん</rt></ruby>はかどらない。
sentences.translation: I'm doing my homework while listening to music, but I'm making zero progress.
sentences.notes: Classic simultaneous-action usage — the <ruby>音楽<rt>おんがく</rt></ruby>を<ruby>聴<rt>き</rt></ruby>く part is the background activity, and doing <ruby>宿題<rt>しゅくだい</rt></ruby> is the main task.

sentences.jp: <ruby>毎朝<rt>まいあさ</rt></ruby>、コーヒーを<ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>みながらニュースをチェックしています。
sentences.translation: Every morning I check the news while drinking coffee.
sentences.notes: A natural daily-routine sentence showing that the coffee-drinking frames the news-checking, not the other way around.

sentences.jp: <ruby>弊社<rt>へいしゃ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>伝統<rt>でんとう</rt></ruby>を<ruby>守<rt>まも</rt></ruby>りながら、<ruby>新<rt>あたら</rt></ruby>しい<ruby>技術<rt>ぎじゅつ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>取<rt>と</rt></ruby>り<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れてまいります。
sentences.translation: Our company will continue to adopt new technologies while preserving our traditions.
sentences.notes: This is a very common formal/business phrasing where ながら conveys 'balancing two things simultaneously,' often used in company statements and speeches.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>悪<rt>わる</rt></ruby>いと<ruby>知<rt>し</rt></ruby>りながら、それをやり<ruby>続<rt>つづ</rt></ruby>けた。
sentences.translation: He kept doing it even though he knew it was wrong.
sentences.notes: This is the 'despite/even though' meaning of ながら — completely different from simultaneous actions; it highlights a contradiction between knowing something and acting against it, carrying a tone of reproach or irony.

confused_with.contrast: Both ～ながら and ～つつ can express simultaneous actions, but ～つつ is distinctly literary and formal — you'll see it in written Japanese, speeches, or set phrases, not in everyday conversation. Compare: <ruby>音楽<rt>おんがく</rt></ruby>を<ruby>聴<rt>き</rt></ruby>きながら<ruby>勉強<rt>べんきょう</rt></ruby>する (natural casual/standard) vs. <ruby>音楽<rt>おんがく</rt></ruby>を<ruby>聴<rt>き</rt></ruby>きつつ<ruby>勉強<rt>べんきょう</rt></ruby>する (stiff, literary). ～つつ also has its own strong 'despite' usage (～つつも) that parallels ながら's contradiction meaning, but again at a higher register. Reach for ながら in speech; use ～つつ when writing formally.

confusion_set.use_when: Two actions happen at the same time (one as background), OR to express 'despite/even though' in a slightly ironic or contradictory context — works in all registers from casual speech to formal writing.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>歩<rt>ある</rt></ruby>きながらスマホを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>るのは<ruby>危<rt>あぶ</rt></ruby>ない。

confusion_set.use_when: Same simultaneous or 'despite' meaning as ながら, but ONLY in formal writing or set expressions — never in casual conversation.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>環境<rt>かんきょう</rt></ruby>を<ruby>守<rt>まも</rt></ruby>りつつ、<ruby>経済<rt>けいざい</rt></ruby><ruby>発展<rt>はってん</rt></ruby>を<ruby>目指<rt>めざ</rt></ruby>す。

confusion_set.use_when: Emphasizes a specific window of time ('during the time that A is happening, B occurs') — focuses on duration and timing rather than one action being a backdrop for another.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>寝<rt>ね</rt></ruby>ているあいだに、プレゼントを<ruby>準備<rt>じゅんび</rt></ruby>した。

bunpro_tip: When you see ながら, ask yourself: 'Is this simultaneous background action, or is this a contradiction?' — BunPro will test both meanings. A quick mnemonic: picture someone walking (background action) and talking (main action) — that's ながら. If the sentence has a moral tension ('he knew better, but...'), that's also ながら, just in its ironic mode.
